entry.title: The Start of Becoming a Better Scientific Writer

link.title: The Start of Becoming a Better Scientific Writer

entry.content:
During my Ph.D I focused on analytical training more than writing about it. I assumed that writing would improve naturally with practice, that intuition would be enough. It wasn’t. Scientific writing took as much time as the analysis itself, yet I approached it without any deliberate training or strategy.

During the final chapter, my focus narrowed to the flaws in the analysis rather than the overall findings. One paragraph even began with “Control sample was excluded from the first sequencing run because of this and that…”. I read that sentence multiple times without recognizing any issue; I was not seeing the forest for the trees. Instead of presenting the results, I started with what went wrong and buried the actual findings under unnecessary details.

If I want to continue doing research, I need to communicate my work clearly. Being merely a good writer is not enough; I need to become an excellent one. I decided to approach writing more deliberately and seek guidance from writing-focused books. I also recognize that many researchers face similar challenges, so I decided to write weekly blog posts documenting my progress, with the hope that others may benefit from this process.
entry.summary: During my Ph.D I focused on analytical training more than writing about it. I assumed that writing would improve naturally with practice, that intuition would be enough. It wasn’t. Scientific writing took as much time as the analysis itself, yet I approached it without any deliberate training or strategy.

entry.title: Wait a sec… Maybe I had a different style of writing

link.title: Wait a sec… Maybe I had a different style of writing

entry.content:
Kurt Vonnegut-Timequake described two kinds of writer:

  1. Swoopers write a story quickly then go over it again painstakingly, fixing every-thing that…doesn`t work: higgledy piggledy - in confusion or disorder., crinkum crankum -  full of twists, turns, or excessive, intricate details [Vonnegut 1997; Heard, 2016]
  2. Basher - go ne sentence at a time, getting it exactly right before they go on the next one. when they are done, they are done. [Vonnegut 1997; Heard, 2016]
  3. Draggers - they think of themselves as bashers, but they are wrong. Their attempts become endless toil as they drag themselves through writing weighed down by a premature search for perfection. Draggers agonize over perfect phrasing - only to find when they write the next page that they need to go back and re-write that perfect phrasing anyway.
entry.summary: Kurt Vonnegut-Timequake described two kinds of writer: Swoopers write a story quickly then go over it again painstakingly, fixing every-thing that…doesn`t work: higgledy piggledy - in confusion or disorder., crinkum crankum - full of twists, turns, or excessive, intricate details [Vonnegut 1997; Heard, 2016] Basher - go ne sentence at a time, getting it exactly right before they go on the next one. when they are done, they are done. [Vonnegut 1997; Heard, 2016] Draggers - they think of themselves as bashers, but they are wrong. Their attempts become endless toil as they drag themselves through writing weighed down by a premature search for perfection. Draggers agonize over perfect phrasing - only to find when they write the next page that they need to go back and re-write that perfect phrasing anyway.
